title: Contractibility of the space of tight contact structures on R^3
speaker: Yakov Eliashberg
affiliation: Stanford University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: 30 years ago I proved that any tight contact structure on the 3-sphere is diffeomorphic to the standard one. I also optimistically claimed at the same paper that similar methods could be used to prove a multi-parametric version: the space of tight contact structures on the 3-sphere, fixed at a point, is contractible. In our recent joint with N. Mishachev paper we proved this result. While the proof indeed roughly follows the strategy of my 1991 paper, it is much more involved.

proof_stages.label: Statement and contact-topological setting
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the uniqueness theorem for tight contact structures on R^3, defines the tight/overtwisted dichotomy, and states the main theorem that the space of tight contact structures on R^3 standard at infinity is contractible, with equivalent S^3 and contactomorphism-group formulations.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic foliations and tightness obstructions on spheres
proof_stages.summary: The proof studies a family of spheres in R^3 via their characteristic line fields, analyzes elliptic, hyperbolic, and birth-death singularities with signs, and uses the same-sign polygon/cancellation criterion to translate tightness into restrictions on the topology of these foliations.

proof_stages.label: Topological criterion and genericity reduction
proof_stages.summary: The argument explains that the homeomorphism type of a sufficiently generic characteristic foliation should decide tightness, compares this with Giroux convex-surface criteria, and invokes a parametric h-principle allowing families of surfaces to be isotoped so that only nondegenerate and birth-death singularities occur.

proof_stages.label: Simple functions and boundary extension
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces simple functions on the 3-ball, simple functions on the boundary sphere, and the restriction map Simple_3 -> Simple_2, whose fibers are nonempty and contractible and which is a Serre fibration, giving canonical extension of simple boundary data up to homotopy.

proof_stages.label: Taming functions for tight characteristic foliations
proof_stages.summary: A taming function is defined as a Lyapunov function whose extrema and hyperbolic signs match those of the characteristic foliation, and the central criterion is that tight characteristic foliations admit simple taming functions, with the space of choices contractible over each foliation.

proof_stages.label: Canonical contact extension over the ball
proof_stages.summary: Given a tight characteristic foliation on S^2, the proof chooses a taming function, extends it as a simple function on B^3, realizes the resulting data locally using an immersion/hypersurface construction in a complex 4-dimensional model, and obtains a contact structure on the ball canonical up to homotopy.

proof_stages.label: Parametric contraction by replacing shells
proof_stages.summary: For a parameterized family of tight contact structures, the radial sphere construction and canonical ball extensions are applied uniformly, then a shrinking-ball homotopy replaces the family by the standard contact structure, proving contractibility of the space.

examples.name: Possible extension to S^2 x S^1
examples.note: The brief mention of simple manifolds such as S^2 x S^1 illustrates that the contactomorphism-group reformulation is not peculiar to R^3, but it is not used in the proof of the R^3 theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He only says the method should apply to simple cases like S^2 × S^1, without analyzing that manifold.

examples.name: Morse-theoretic cancellation contrast
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the contact cancellation of an elliptic-hyperbolic pair along a characteristic trajectory with ordinary cancellation of critical points of a function along a gradient trajectory, to make the contact-geometric move more intelligible.
examples.engagement_reason: He contrasts the contact cancellation move with Morse cancellation by reasoning through why a gradient trajectory from a hyperbolic point to an elliptic point cannot occur in the Morse/function picture.

passes.reason: The Morse cancellation picture is a standard generic comparison for critical-point cancellation, used only as contrast rather than to refute a claim.
The Morse-cancellation discussion is a standard comparison with ordinary Morse theory rather than a structurally special object or a disproof.
The Morse cancellation situation is used only as a standard comparison with the contact cancellation lemma, not as an adversarial example.
Morse cancellation is used only as a standard nondegenerate critical-point contrast to the contact cancellation lemma.
The Morse cancellation setup is a standard comparison with ordinary nondegenerate critical-point cancellation, used only as contrast.
Ordinary Morse cancellation is used only as a contrast with contact-geometric cancellation.
The Morse cancellation picture is used to show that the usual Morse-theoretic intuition does not carry over to the contact cancellation lemma.

Ordinary Morse cancellation is invoked only as a familiar contrast to the contact cancellation lemma, with generic nondegenerate Morse data.
Ordinary Morse critical-point behavior is used to show the analogous elliptic-hyperbolic trajectory/cancellation cannot occur there.
ordinary Morse cancellation is used only as a contrast with the contact-foliation cancellation lemma.
Ordinary Morse cancellation is invoked only as a contrasting familiar model, with standard nondegenerate critical points.

examples.name: Igusa-style generalized Morse functions
examples.note: The known h-principle for families of functions with only Morse and birth-death singularities is presented as the model that leads to the analogous genericity statement for characteristic foliations of surfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: He discusses generalized Morse functions as a tool, spelling out family deformation, boundary conditions, and the resulting simple-function spaces used in the proof.

passes.reason: Igusa-style generalized Morse functions allow birth-death singularities, i.e. controlled degeneracies of ordinary Morse critical points, and are used as an analogy.
Generalized Morse functions allow birth-death singularities, which are controlled degenerate limiting cases, and are used as an analogy/tool rather than a counterexample.

Igusa-style generalized Morse functions are used as a positive model and allow controlled birth-death degeneracies.

examples.name: Non-simple level sets in a pushed-in ball
examples.note: A ball whose level surface acquires an annular component, contrasted with cactus-like contractible level components, makes concrete the definition of simple functions and later the obstruction to a simple taming function.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives the pushed-in ball as a concrete non-simple case and checks the condition by observing that a level-set component is an annulus rather than contractible.

passes.reason: The pushed-in ball gives a level-set component such as an annulus, explicitly showing failure of the stated simplicity/contractibility condition.
